title: Periodic and Aperiodic Signals
summary: This page introduces the classification of signals as periodic or aperiodic, defining a periodic signal x(t) as one satisfying x(t) = x(t + T0) for all t, where T0 is the fundamental period. It also illustrates continuous-time and discrete-time signals through Figure 1.10, using a GNP chart as an example of a discrete-time signal. The page notes that periodic signals must start at t = -∞ by definition.

figures.caption: Examples of (a) a continuous-time signal x(t) and (b) a discrete-time signal representing quarterly GNP percent change from 1981 to 1994, illustrating the return of recession.
figures.description: The figure has two panels comparing signal types. In (a), the horizontal axis is time t and the vertical axis is x(t); a smooth continuous curve rises from 0, peaks, crosses the horizontal axis, and then decays slightly below zero, showing a continuous-time signal. In (b), a discrete-time bar chart titled "Quarterly GNP: The return of recession" plots percent change on the vertical axis versus years 1981 to 1994 on the horizontal axis, with bars above and below a zero line. Students should notice the contrast between a continuous waveform and sampled quarterly economic data, and how negative bars mark recession periods highlighted by annotations.
